file_name: GSITECHNOLOGYINC_11_16_2009-EX-10.2-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between SONY ELECTRONICS INC. and GSI TECHNOLOGY, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: From time to time, as and when requested by either Party, the other Party will execute and deliver, or cause to be executed and delivered, all such documents and instruments, make such other deliveries and take such other actions as may be reasonably necessary to consummate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: From time to time, as and when requested by either Party, the other Party will endeavor to execute and deliver, or cause to be executed and delivered, documents and instruments, make deliveries and take actions, to help consummate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may be reasonably necessary' to a simple 'help consummate' weakens the obligation, making it unclear what level of effort is required. The original text suggests a reasonable necessity standard, but the replacement introduces ambiguity about the extent of cooperation required, potentially allowing a party to avoid fulfilling their obligations under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1655 states that 'if the terms of a promise are in any respect ambiguous or uncertain, it must be interpreted in the sense in which the promisor believed, at the time of making it, that the promisee understood it.' By making the obligation to execute documents and take actions less definitive, the modified text creates ambiguity that could lead to disputes about the parties' understood intent and the level of effort required, conflicting with the code's aim for clarity and mutual understanding.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the original obligation by changing 'may be reasonably necessary' to 'help consummate,' creating ambiguity about the required effort. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1655, as interpreted in the Law Explanation, which emphasizes clarity and mutual understanding in contractual terms. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 1655, reinforces this by stating that stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable are implied, suggesting that the original text's 'reasonably necessary' standard aligns better with legal expectations than the ambiguous 'help consummate.' The Perturbation Explanation indicates that this change allows a party to potentially avoid fulfilling their obligations, creating a contradiction with the law's intent to ensure reasonable and enforceable agreements.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and, unless otherwise provided in this IP Agreement, will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered in person or when dispatched by electronic facsimile transfer (confirmed in writing by mail simultaneously dispatched) or one business day after having been dispatched by a nationally recognized overnight courier service to the appropriate Party at the address specified below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications will ideally be in writing and may be considered given when delivered or dispatched by means considered reasonable at the time.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces several ambiguities. Removing the specific methods of delivery (in person, fax, courier) and replacing them with 'means considered reasonable at the time' makes it unclear what constitutes proper notice. This opens the door for disputes about whether a particular method (e.g., email without confirmation) is acceptable, potentially undermining the enforceability of the agreement. This also removes the requirement that faxes be confirmed in writing by mail, weakening the assurance of receipt.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1201 defines when a person has notice of a fact, and it generally requires actual knowledge or receipt of a notification. By replacing the specific notice provisions with a vague 'means considered reasonable,' the modified text creates uncertainty about when notice is legally effective. This could allow a party to argue they didn't receive proper notice even if a reasonable attempt was made, potentially undermining the clear notice requirements often required to trigger actions or deadlines within a contract. The original requirement of written notice and specific delivery methods aimed to provide certainty, while the new language allows for more subjective interpretations, potentially conflicting with requirements for clear communication within business transactions.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessi
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding proper notice, potentially conflicting with California Commercial Code § 1201, which necessitates clear communication. The original text specified delivery methods to ensure certainty, whereas the new language relies on subjective interpretation, weakening assurance of receipt. The law snippet 1 defines key terms to ensure clarity. By replacing specific methods with 'means considered reasonable,' the enforceability is undermined.

perturbation.original_text: SONY has the sole right to enforce all of the Transferred Patents and the other SRAM Intellectual Property rights of SONY included in the Transferred Assets.
perturbation.changed_text: SONY generally retains the right to enforce the Transferred Patents and other SRAM Intellectual Property rights.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'has the sole right to enforce' to 'generally retains the right to enforce', the modification introduces ambiguity about whether SONY's right is exclusive or subject to exceptions. This could imply that PURCHASER or another third party might also have some enforcement rights, creating confusion and potential disputes over who is responsible for protecting the intellectual property. It also weakens the original text which firmly places the right of enforcement on SONY.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property and that 'applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing.' While this section speaks more generally about assignment, ownership carries with it the right to exclude others and enforce the patent. The original text clearly assigned that right to SONY but the modified text introduces uncertainty. To the degree the 'general' right implies that other parties, including perhaps PURCHASER, could be construed to also have enforcement rights, this introduces ambiguity and potential conflict over who controls this enforcement, undermining the clear property right structure intended by patent law and potentially impacting the value of the transferred assets.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982
perturbation.justification: The original text assigns SONY the 'sole right to enforce' transferred patents. The modified text states SONY 'generally retains the right to enforce,' which introduces ambiguity. 35 U.S. Code § 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property and are assignable. While the snippet focuses on assignment, it implies that ownership includes the right to exclude others, which the original text clearly granted to SONY. The modified text weakens this right by suggesting that others might also have enforcement rights, contradicting the implication of exclusive ownership inherent in patent law and undermining the clear property right structure intended by patent law. The change from 'sole' to 'generally' creates a loophole where other parties could claim enforcement rights, potentially diminishing the value and control of the transferred assets. The law snippet reinforces the concept of assignable ownership, which is compromised by the ambiguous language introduced in the modified text.
